pages.text: Micromechanical modeling on autogenous and drying shrinkages of concrete Jun Zhang ⇑, Dongwei Hou, Yudong Han Key Laboratory of Safety and Durability of Civil Engineering of China Education Ministry, Department of Civil Engineering, Tsinghua University, Be ijing 100084, China article info Article history: Received 6 May 2011 Received in revised form 14 September 2011 Accepted 29 September 2011 Available online 24 November 2011 Keywords: Model Autogenous shrinkage Drying shrinkage Relative humidity abstract A micromechanical model for autogenous and drying shrinkage predictions of concrete at early-age is developed. The model is based on the capillary tension created in capillary pores in concrete and uses the interior humidity (RH) that can relate to the moisture loss in concrete directly as driving parameter for shrinkage predictions. The developed model can be used for shrinkage prediction of concrete, what- ever the shrinkage is caused by self-desiccation or by dying. In addition, because the model can reﬂect the effect on moisture loss of age and position from one parameter, RH, the model can predict shrinkage strain in concrete structures not only for different time but also for different positions. To verify the model, a series shrinkage tests on three types of concrete with compressive strength around 30 MPa, 50 MPa and 80 MPa respectively at 28 days of moisture curing were carried out. The model predictions on the development of shrinkage strain are compared with experimental results and a good agreement between them is found. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Shrinkage of concrete is one of the major mechanisms leading to the initial crack formation in concrete structures. Concrete shrinks as moisture is lost to the environment or by self-desicca- tion. As concrete shrinks, a certain amount of tensile stresses will be developed in structure due to restraints from adjunct materials or connected members. The stresses may exceed the tensile strength and cause concrete to crack. Cracking in concrete mem- bers reduces the load carrying capacity of the structure. Moreover, cracks allow water and other chemical agents, such as deicing salt, to go through the cover layer to come into contact with the rein- forcements, leading to reinforcement corrosion and rupture in steel reinforced concrete. This is particularly true in the case of concrete overlays and slabs on grade where larger surface areas are exposed to drying. The magnitude of the shrinkage strain is normally proportional to the amount of moisture loss [1–4].I n general, environmental drying and cement hydration are the two main processes leading the moisture inside of concrete to loss. As environmental humidity is lower than the humidity inside of con- crete, water in concrete evaporates and shrinkage of concrete arises. This kind of shrinkage is normally called drying shrinkage. Another process leading the moisture loss is through cement hydration, which is so called self-desiccation and the correspond- ing shrinkage is called autogenous shrinkage. As a main mechanism leading to concrete cracking, extensive studies on shrinkage of concrete have been carried out. Drying shrinkage has been intensively studied since Forties of the last Century and autogenous shrinkage has been a hot research topic in the last decade with the development of high performance con- crete [5–9]. Normally the autogenous shrinkage and drying shrink- age are investigated sepa

humidity in early-age concrete [5]. Typical experimental results are displayed in Fig. 1, where the C30 and C80 respectively denotes the concrete having compressive strength of 30 MPa and 80 MPa after 28 days of moisture curing. The applicable of the shrinkage model based on interior humidity for both drying and autogenous shrinkages prediction is certainly of signiﬁcant importance, espe- cially for application on concrete. The purpose of this paper is to improve the knowledge of con- crete material and to contribute to understanding and predicting of shrinkage of concrete in early age, including both autogenous and drying shrinkages. In the study, an internal humidity based analytical micro-mechanical model for prediction of concrete shrinkage is developed. The model relies on the chemical shrinkage and relative humidity that relates to the interior moisture loss, and therefore the model can be used for shrinkage prediction whatever the moisture loss is caused by drying or by cement hydration. In order to verify the model, the shrinkage of concrete specimens under both fully plastic ﬁlm sealing and ﬁve faces drying curing conditions is experimentally investigated by measuring deforma- tion, interior humidity and temperature simultaneously. The global deformation, interior humidity and temperature were immediately measured after a few hours of the specimen cast until 28 days. The effect of concrete strength on the development of deformation, interior humidity and inside temperature with concrete age is evaluated and discussed by using three types of concretes. Finally, the model predictions and experimental results is compared each other. A reasonable agreement between theoretical predictions and experimental results is obtained and conclusions are drawn at the end of the paper. 2. Development of the model In fresh concrete, all pores between cement and other solid par- ticles are initially ﬁlled with water and a few air bubbles. After set- ting of fresh concrete, a stiff skeleton is formed and the chemical contraction produced by cement hydration can not completely transfer to macroscopic shrinkage of concrete. Therefore, with con- tinuation of cement hydration, a number of capillary pores be- tween cement particles are gradually formed and corresponding meniscuses are created to compensate the volume decrease. The generation of capillary pores during cement hydration is illustrated by Fig. 2, that initially proposed by Jensen and Hansen [17]. Mean- while, the interior humidity of concrete starts to decrease from the initial saturated state of 100% because the continuity of liquid water gradually destroyed with the formation of capillary pores. According to Kelvin’s law, the relationship between relative humidity (RH), radius of meniscus r, surface tension of water c and absolute temperature T can be expressed as: r ¼/C0 2cM lnðRHÞqRT ð1Þ where M is molar weight of water (0.01802 kg/mol), q is density of water and R is ideal gas constant (8.314 J/mol K). Obviously, the meniscus radius formed inside the capillary pores is reduced with decrease of relative humidity. Due to the formation of capillary pores, the stress balance between inside and outside of cement paste is destroyed and this nonequilibrium state leads the matrix undergoes depression. According to Laplace equation, the tensile stress leading to concrete shrinkage can be calculated by: rw ¼/C0 lnðRHÞqRT M ð2Þ Then the next we need to do is to calculate the resulting strain fr

(23), we obtain the relationship between cement hydration rate and interior relative humidity as: da dte ¼ ac /C1B A ln au a /C16/C17/C16/C17 Bþ1 B /C0 P ! ðRHÞn þ P ð26Þ Constants n and P can be determined from interior humidity measurements and isothermal tests. Thus for different drying pro- cess, the effect of interior humidity variation on cement hydration can be estimated by Eq. (26). The cement hydration degree at given time te can be obtained by integrating formula (26) from 0 to te. As shown in the model, elastic modulus of concrete is also an important parameter for shrinkage calculation. After set, the elastic modulus of concrete starts to develop from zero. Based on the equivalent age, the development of elastic modulus of concrete with age under varied temperature and drying status can be esti- mated by [26–28]: EðaÞ¼ 1:05E28 a /C0 a0 au /C0 a0 /C18/C19 b ð27Þ where a0 is the hydration degree at concrete setting. b is a constant that can be determined by ﬁtting Eq. (27) with experimental data. So far, as long as the cement hydration degree and interior rel- ative humidity are known, the shrinkage strain produced from moisture loss can be calculated by Eq. (12). However, Eq. (12) can only calculate the shrinkage strain occurred in the humidity reduction stage. Previous studies have found that the variation law of the interior relative humidity of concrete with age since concrete cast can be described by a water–vapor saturated stage with 100% relative humidity followed by a stage that relative humidity gradually decreases [5,26]. To calculate the shrinkage strain developed in the moisture saturated stage after setting, a complementary model is needed. As state above, the deformation developed in the moisture saturated stage is principally controlled by the chemical shrinkage developed in this period. Because the stiffness at this age is relative low, the macroscopic shrinkage strain should have closed correlation with chemical shrinkage. As- sume the chemical shrinkage produced at a given time after con- crete setting is ( V cs–Vcs0) (in volume), the inﬂuencing factor of stiffness is g, thus the shrinkage strain present in length change can be estimated by: ew ¼ g 1 /C0 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 1 /C0ð Vcs /C0 Vcs0Þ 3 phi ð28Þ where Vcs0 is the chemical shrinkage (in volume) at the point that the interior humidity starts to decrease from 100% and it can be cal- culated using ac in Eqs. (13) or (15) for normal concrete and high strength concrete with silica fume addition respectively. Thus, the complete model for prediction of shrinkage developed in both mois- ture saturated period and humidity reduction period can be given by combining formulas (12) and (28) as: ew ¼ g 1 /C0 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 1 /C0ð Vcs /C0 Vcs0Þ3 p/C2/C3 for RH ¼ 1 g 1 /C0 ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ 1 /C0ð Vcs /C0 Vcs0Þ3 p/C2/C3 þ Smp qRT 3M 1 Ks /C0 1 K /C16/C17 lnðRHÞ for RH < 1 8 < : ð29Þ The shrinkage model developed above is based on the formation of capillary pores and capillary stresses during the formation of ce- ment matrix skeleton. Therefore, the model can be used for mois- ture loss resulted shrinkage prediction regardless if the moisture loss is caused by cement hydration or by environmental drying. Further, by applying present model, the calculation of shrinkage distribution in concrete structures in the case of humidity gradient existing becomes possible. In addition, the inﬂuences of curing conditions, includin

the surfaces, the casting face was covered with soft plastic sheet to prevent mois- ture exchange with its surroundings. About 2 h after casting, the steel bar was re- moved from the tube and it was veriﬁed that there was no liquid water left inside the tube before the humidity sensor was inserted. Meanwhile, the four removable thin sheets were slowly lifted and a 2 mm gap between specimen and mold was created after initial set of the concrete. Afterward, the two LVDTs were installed at the two long ends of the specimen. The deformation was measured as the horizontal movement of the two bars placed centrally in both ends of the spec- imen. During the test, the humidity and temperature were automatically recorded every 10 min by a personal computer. In order to investigate the shrinkage behavior under both sealing and drying conditions, two identical specimens were cast for each concrete. After 3 days of concrete casting, the plastic ﬁle covering the ﬁve sur- faces of the one specimen was removed to simulate the drying condition. While the other specimen keeps the sealing state to compare with the drying specimen. A photo showing the two parallel specimens under testing is presented in Fig. 8 . The tests were continued until 28 days since concrete casting. All tests were carried out in a laboratory under room temperature. 4. Typical test result of the develop- ment of interior humidity, global deformation and interior temper- ature with ages since concrete casting are shown in Fig. 9 a–c, where Fig. 9 c displays the initial part of the development of dis- placement with age. From the results shown in Fig. 9 , ﬁrst we can observe again that the general behavior of the interior relative humidity of the concrete since the time of casting can be described by a water–vapor saturated stage with 100% relative humidity (a) A 3 mm2 mm B (b) Fig. 6. (stage I) followed by a stage in which the relative humidity is grad- ually decreasing (stage II) [5,26]. Second, looking at the global deformation–time curves shown in Fig. 9 and more detailed in Fig. 10, the free deformation of concrete at very early ages is char- acterized by ﬁrst swelling and then shrinking. This deformation behavior at early age was also found by Kamen [29] on ultra-high performance ﬁber reinforced concrete and by Sule et al. and Bentur et al. respectively on conventional concrete [30,31]. After achieving a peak of swelling, the specimen starts to shrink with a gradually decreased rate. The mechanism of above deformation characteris- tic of concrete at early age may be analyzed as follows. At the beginning after concrete casting, all volume changes produced by cement hydration, moisture diffusion and by temperature varia- tion result in plastic settlement, which in turn, produces lateral swelling and top shrinking in generally because at this moment the concrete behaves as a plastic material [5,32]. After a period of time, a combination of particle interference and hydration prod- ucts gives stiffness which is sufﬁcient to make the concrete support its own weight. In addition, the capillary stresses shown in Fig. 4 is ideal situa- tion, in concrete the action area of the stress in capillary pores are smaller than that shown in the ﬁgure and this inﬂuence can be ta- ken into account by introducing a capillary saturation factor. Con- sidering the effect of saturation degree of capillary pores [15], Eq. (11) can be modiﬁed as: ew ¼/C0 SmpqRT 3M 1 K /C0 1 Ks /C18/C19 lnðRHÞð 12Þ where S is saturation fraction, S = Vew/Vp. Vew is the evaporable water content in the hardening cement paste, Vp is the total pore volume. The saturation fraction S can be estimated through Powers’ volumetric models [19,20]. Assuming the hydration degree of ce- ment is a, and the total volume of cement particles and water is 1, the phase composition of a hardening Portland cement paste without silica fume addition, including chemical shrinkage Vcs, cap- illary pore water volume Vcw, gel water volume Vgw, gel solid vol- ume Vgs and unhydrated cement volume Vc can be calculated through the following equation: Vcs ¼ 0:2ð1 /C0 pÞa Vcw ¼ p /C0 1:3ð1 /C0 pÞa Vgw ¼ 0:6ð1 /C0 pÞa Vgs ¼ 1:5ð1 /C0 pÞa Vc ¼ð 1 /C0 pÞð1 /C0 aÞ 8 >> >>> > < >>>> > >: ð13Þ where RVi =1 , p ¼ w=c w=cþqw =qc . w and c are the weight of water and ce- ment respectively in concrete mixture. qw and qc are density of water and cement respectively. So the saturation fraction S can be calculated by: S ¼ p /C0 0:7ð1 /C0 pÞa p /C0 0:5ð1 /C0 pÞa ð14Þ The above equations can be used only in the shrinkage calcula- tion for concrete without silica fume application. For the concrete with silica fume addition, the phase composition, including chem- ical shrinkage V cs, capillary pore water volume Vcw, gel water vol- ume Vgw, gel solid volume Vgs and unhydrated cement particles volume Vc as well as silica fume volume Vs can be estimated by [8]: Vcs ¼ kð0:2 þ 0:7 s cÞð1 /C0 pÞa Vcw ¼ p /C0 kð1:4 þ 1:6 s cÞð1 /C0 pÞa Vgw ¼ kð0:6 þ 1:6 s cÞð1 /C0 pÞa Vgs ¼ kð1:6 þ 0:7 s cÞð1 /C0 pÞa Vc ¼ kð1 /C0 pÞð1 /C0 aÞ Vs ¼ kð1:4 s cÞð1 /C0 pÞð1 /C0 aÞ 8 >>>> > >>> > < >>>> > >>> > : ð15Þ where RVi =1 , s, c are the weight of silica fume and cement respec- tively. p ¼ w=c w=cþqw =qc þðqw =qc Þðs=cÞ ; k ¼ 1 1þ1:4ðs=cÞ. Thus, the saturation fraction S for concrete with silica fume addition can be calculated by: S ¼ p /C0 0:8kð1 /C0 pÞa p /C0 k½0:6 /C0 0:7ðs=cÞ/C138ð1 /C0 pÞa ð16Þ In present work, isothermal tests was used to calculate the ce- ment hydration degree, a. By measuring the adiabatic temperature rise of concrete at different time, the cement hydration degree is estimated by: a ¼ TadðtÞ Tadð1Þ au ð17Þ where Tad (t) is the adiabatic temperature rising at time t, Tad(1)i s the ultimate adiabatic temperature rising. au is ultimate degree of hydration and is a function of water to cement ratio ( w/c)a s [21]: au ¼ 1:031w=c 0:194 þ w=c ð18Þ In order to calculate hydration degree under different tempera- ture history, a concept called equivalent age is used [21]. The equivalent age concept assumes that samples of a concrete mixture of the same equivalent age will have the same mechanical proper- ties or cement hydration degree, regardless of the combination of time and temperature yielding the equivalent age. Based on above deﬁnition, the equivalent age te can be expressed as te ¼ Z t 0 e 1 R Uar 293/C0 UaT 273þT /C0/C1 dt ð19Þ where te is the equivalent age at the reference temperature (here the reference temperature is equal to 20 /C176C is assumed). Uar and UaT are the apparent acti

humidity and shrinkage of the two parallel specimens of each kind of concrete are almost identical. As one of the two specimens is starting to dry at 3 days, a fast reduction in the interior humidity immediately occurs, and accordingly, a fast increase in the shrink- age happens accompanying the reduction of the interior humidity. The difference between drying and sealing specimens on both inte- rior humidity and shrinkage gradually increases with concrete age. At 28 days, the interior humidity under sealing and drying condi- tions for C30, C50 and C80 concretes are 92%, 85%, 80% and 60%, 69%, 59% respectively. Accordingly, the shrinkage under sealing and drying conditions for C30, C50 and C80 concretes at 28 days are 112 /C2 10/C0 6, 332 /C2 10/C0 6, 526 /C2 10/C0 6 and 322 /C2 10/C0 6, 822 /C2 10/C0 6, 756 /C2 10/C0 6 respectively. Apparently, moisture loss presented by interior humidity can be well correlated with shrinkage of con- crete and the interior humidity can serve as the internal driving force of shrinking modeling. 4.1. Comparison of model and test results To verify the model, the development of shrinkage trains caused by moisture loss under plastic sealing and drying conditions respectively of C30, C50 and C80 concretes are calculated using the model.
